You're probably itching to get into more techniques for uncovering black swans, but first, let's discuss the concept of leverage, the mystical commodity everyone seems to be desperate for. Leverage is one of those buzz words that commentators love to throw out, but rarely delve into what it really means. In theory, leverage is the ability to inflict loss and withhold gain. But in reality, the black swan group says there's always leverage. Jim Camp, who wrote Start With No, says there's no such thing as leverage. One of my colleagues from Harvard once said it doesn't matter what leverage they have on you, what matters is what do they think of the leverage you have on them. When we wrote the book, we took a deep dive into the three types of leverage, positive, negative, and normative. Let's skip all that. In reality, leverage is a form of perceived influence. It's based on vision, the vision of the beholder. Leverage, like beauty, is in the eye of the beholder. What does your counterpart see as its stake? What do they want to gain and what do they fear losing most? What do you see as its stake? What do you fear losing most? The answers to those questions are the keys to what's perceived as leverage. If they don't recognize what you think is leverage, no rational approach will change that perspective. There's a huge distinction between what someone fears losing versus what someone is actually going to lose or in the process of losing. In the book Barbarians at the Gate, Shearson Lehman was bidding against KKR, Colberg, Kravitz, Roberts, to buy out RJR Nabisco. KKR offered Shearson Lehman over $100 million to simply step out of the bidding to back away, $100 million to do nothing. Lehman turned it down. They feared that accepting $100 million might make them look bad in the mergers and acquisitions industry. Did they stop to consider that if someone would offer them over $100 million just to get out of the way, that party would likely end up outbidding them anyway? And because of that, were they willing to consider they were probably already losing? Well, what happened? KKR won. And Shearson Lehman? They abandoned that line of business entirely. They ended up with no reputation. They surrendered for no compensation, something they could have accepted over $100 million for. What people fear they have to lose will have three to nine times more impact than the value of what they hope to gain. Daniel Kahneman's Nobel Prize-winning prospect theory, which we've discussed earlier in these courses, says lost things twice as much as an equivalent gain. Professor Kahneman once gave an interview when he said he and his partner Amos Tversky actually believed that lost things five to seven times as much as gain. They just lowered the number to two times because they wanted fewer arguments from their colleagues. There are sales programs that show you have to show nine times value to make the sale. We've heard data that says that 70% of buy decisions are made to avoid loss. In any event, the prospect of loss is a much bigger emotional impact on decision making and therefore the concept of leverage then gain. Leverage is an emotional concept and emotions are always present. Let me ask you this quick question. Who do you think has the leverage in a kidnapping? The kidnapper or the victim's family?
